Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Moral Status & AI Ethics

Martha Nussbaum

b. 1947

Capabilities, emotion, and the ethical significance of vulnerable minds.

Martha Nussbaum did not write a single systematic treatise called “philosophy of mind.” The right procedure is therefore reconstructive. Her philosophy of mind has to be extracted from several interconnected bodies of work: her Aristotelian scholarship on De Motu Animalium and De Anima; her theory of emotion in The Fragility of Goodness, The Therapy of Desire, Upheavals of Thought, Hiding from Humanity, and Anger and Forgiveness; her moral psychology of literature in Love’s Knowledge and Poetic Justice; her political theory of capabilities; her later work on animal minds and animal justice; and her writings on religion, conscience, fear, and political emotion. Her official academic profile itself reflects this breadth: ancient Greek and Roman philosophy, feminist philosophy, political philosophy, philosophy and literature, law, divinity, classics, political science, and South Asian studies all enter the intellectual formation of the view.

The thesis that emerges is this: for Nussbaum, mind or soul is not a detachable immaterial substance and not a private Cartesian theater. It is the embodied, affective, evaluative, imaginative, desiring, practically oriented life of a vulnerable creature. Consciousness is not exhausted by propositional thought or self-conscious metacognition; it is a subjective point of view on the world, saturated with salience, desire, aversion, pleasure, distress, and striving. Emotion is not an irrational intrusion into cognition but one of cognition’s central forms: an appraisal of what matters for flourishing. A Nussbaumian philosophy of mind is therefore an ethics-first and life-form-centered philosophy of mind. It asks not only “what is consciousness?” but also “what sort of creature is conscious?”, “what does the world matter to it?”, “what wounds or nourishes its form of life?”, and “what institutions allow its mental and affective powers to flourish?”

The theological dimension is not incidental. Nussbaum’s writings repeatedly return to the contrast between human vulnerability and fantasies of divine invulnerability, between embodied animal need and aspirations to purification, between political respect for conscience and metaphysical neutrality about the soul, and between religious fear and religiously shaped love, justice, and compassion. Her thought is not confessional theology, but it contains a sustained philosophical anthropology with obvious theological stakes.

1. The Aristotelian starting point: soul as embodied life-form

Nussbaum’s earliest major contribution to this area is her work on Aristotle’s De Motu Animalium. The Holberg Prize profile describes her 1978 work on De Motu Animalium as “a seminal work on Aristotelian philosophy of mind and nature,” and this is not merely a historical point: the categories she develops there continue to govern her later work on emotion, action, and animals.

The central Aristotelian inheritance is that psychē—usually translated “soul”—is not best understood as a ghostly entity inside the body. It is the form, organization, and living activity of a creature. Nussbaum and Hilary Putnam’s “Changing Aristotle’s Mind” is especially important here. They argue against both reductive materialism and the view, associated with Myles Burnyeat, that Aristotle’s account of perception requires no material change. Their Aristotle is neither a Democritean reductionist nor a Platonist dualist; mental life is embodied and materially realized, but it cannot be described adequately by microphysical vocabulary alone.

This matters for the philosophy of mind because it yields a non-Cartesian naturalism. The mind is not outside nature, but neither is it explained away by a thin physicalism. A living organism is a structured unity whose capacities—perceiving, desiring, imagining, moving, loving, fearing, reasoning—belong to the whole creature. The “soul” is the organism’s animating organization, not a separable object.

The same Aristotelian problem appears in De Motu: how does soul move body? A contemporary review of the De Motu literature describes Aristotle’s question as the problem of how mental states representing desired or unwanted objects become bodily movement. Perception, imagination, desire, practical reasoning, emotion, and bodily change are all part of one explanatory field. The mechanism is not supernatural; it concerns how an animal as a living whole is moved by what appears good or bad.

The resulting Nussbaumian view is best called hylomorphic, embodied, and teleological. “Hylomorphic” means that mental life is the form of an organized body, not a second substance. “Embodied” means that there is no mind apart from the vulnerable body and its activities. “Teleological” means that mental life is intelligible in relation to the goods, aims, and characteristic activities of a living creature. This is not a crude biological determinism. It is a view about life as purposive, striving, and normatively structured.

This also explains why Nussbaum can move so naturally from Aristotle to animals. In her later interview about Justice for Animals, she says that she began with Aristotle’s De Motu Animalium and its account of human and animal movement through perception and desire. That continuity between human and nonhuman animal agency remains fundamental to her mature position.

2. Mind as evaluative orientation, not neutral representation

A Nussbaumian philosophy of mind begins not with detached representation but with evaluative orientation. Creatures do not first encounter a neutral world and then add value to it. Rather, to perceive as a living being is already to find the world salient: inviting, threatening, nourishing, dangerous, lovable, hateful, grievable, shameful, or hopeful.

This is clearest in her theory of emotion. In Upheavals of Thought, she argues that emotions involve judgments about things important for a person’s flourishing. They are appraisals of objects taken to matter deeply for well-being; they reveal the agent’s dependence on parts of the world not fully under the agent’s control.

Her account is “cognitive” in a broad sense, but not in the narrow sense that every emotion is a sentence silently uttered in the head. In an interview in Phenomenology and Mind, she describes emotions as appraisals of an object as good or bad for a creature. They are distinct from mere bodily sensations or appetites because they are about something. She also gives them an evolutionary role: emotions function like urgent reports about what matters.

Several non-English discussions correctly emphasize this point. A Portuguese study describes her theory as cognitive-evaluative and neo-Stoic, centered on intentionality and eudaimonistic judgment, while also stressing the democratic importance of emotional education. An Italian source likewise emphasizes that, for Nussbaum, emotions are not blind irrational forces but intentional cognitive elaborations, though not necessarily voluntary or fully conscious.

The phrase “eudaimonistic judgment” needs care. Nussbaum does not mean selfish judgment. She means that an emotion appraises something in relation to the agent’s conception of flourishing. Love appraises another person as central to one’s life; grief appraises a loss as devastating because the lost person mattered; fear appraises something as threatening; compassion appraises another’s suffering as significant. The “self” involved is therefore porous. The beloved, the friend, the child, the political community, the animal, the work of justice, or even the natural world can become part of what the agent experiences as constitutive of a flourishing life.

Nussbaum’s account also refuses the familiar opposition between emotion and reason. The subtitle of Upheavals of Thought—“The Intelligence of Emotions”—is programmatic. The book argues that emotions are not alien forces but discriminating responses to value and importance, and that no adequate ethical theory can ignore them.

This is one of the places where her philosophy of mind becomes directly ethical. Mental life is not simply a mechanism for processing information. It is a field of concern. To understand a mind, one must understand what matters to it.

3. Emotion, judgment, imagination, and feeling

Nussbaum’s emotion theory is often summarized as “emotions are judgments,” but that formulation can mislead unless expanded. Her mature view contains at least four components: intentionality, evaluation, imagination, and affective embodiment.

First, emotions are intentional: they are about objects. Fear is fear of something, grief is grief over someone or something, anger is anger at a perceived wrong, compassion is compassion for another’s suffering. Second, they are evaluative: the object is seen as significant for flourishing or ill-being. Third, they are imaginative: they often involve a concrete “seeing” or “picturing” of the object, not just abstract belief. A French discussion of Nussbaum’s work emphasizes that in Upheavals of Thought the concrete imagining of the emotional object becomes an additional cognitive element, while in Love’s Knowledge Aristotelian phantasia and emotion are essential to practical judgment.

Fourth, emotions are embodied, though Nussbaum resists reducing them to bodily feelings. She does not deny tears, trembling, heat, nausea, or bodily pain. Her claim is that such phenomena do not by themselves identify the emotion. The same bodily arousal can accompany different emotions, while the same emotion can manifest differently across persons and cultures. What makes grief grief is not a bodily sensation alone, but the apprehension of a significant loss.

There is a legitimate criticism here. A Spanish article on Aristotle and Nussbaum argues that her thesis can overreach when she makes feelings depend too strongly on beliefs or judgments, and it questions whether calling emotions true or false is always faithful to Aristotle. This critique is important because it presses Nussbaum’s theory on precisely the point where it risks intellectualizing emotion.

The best reconstruction is therefore this: Nussbaum is not saying that emotions are cold propositions. She is saying that emotions are intelligent, world-directed, value-laden modes of apprehension. They may be linguistically formulated in adults, but they may also exist in infants and animals in nonlinguistic, imagistic, perceptual, and bodily forms. This is why her theory can extend from human moral psychology to animal sentience.

4. Consciousness as subjective point of view and salience

Nussbaum’s most explicit recent account of consciousness appears in her animal ethics. In “Sentience and Striving in a Theory of Justice for Animals,” she defines sentience more broadly than the capacity to feel pain. Sentience is a subjective point of view on the world. It includes the fact that the world looks or feels a certain way to a creature, and it involves perception, desire, movement, pleasure, distress, and affective orientation.

This is philosophically important because it separates consciousness from metacognition. Nussbaum argues that metacognition is only a small part of conscious awareness and is not necessary for pain or other subjective experiences. A creature need not think “I am now having a painful experience” in order to suffer. It is enough that the creature has a felt, subjective experience of distress, threat, frustration, or injury.

Her account also emphasizes significance. Sentience is not a bare sensory field. It includes what she calls the “oomph” of things: the way some features of the world matter, attract, repel, alarm, or satisfy. This makes her view close to a phenomenology of salience. Consciousness is not a screen on which neutral data appear; it is the lived field of a striving creature.

Nussbaum’s criterion for morally relevant consciousness is therefore neither linguistic rationality nor human-like self-awareness. It is significant striving: the active, subjective pursuit of goods and avoidance of bads. She argues that animals capable of perception, desire, and movement inhabit a world that looks some way to them and toward which they are affectively and practically oriented.

This is a major contribution to philosophy of mind because it displaces the human adult as the paradigm of consciousness. Infants, many animals, and cognitively disabled human beings cannot be treated as deficient versions of the rational, verbally articulate adult. Their forms of consciousness are different, but not therefore morally negligible.

5. Desire, movement, and practical agency

Nussbaum’s philosophy of mind is also a theory of agency. In the Aristotelian frame, action is not caused by a detached faculty called “will.” Movement arises when perception, imagination, desire, and practical orientation converge around an apparent good.

In the animal justice article, she describes desire as a felt orientation toward a good and aversion as orientation away from bad. Perception is not passive reception. It is already involved in a creature’s practical life: the world appears as containing things to approach, flee, eat, protect, mourn, mate with, play with, avoid, or explore.

The practical syllogism in Aristotle is crucial here. It is not merely a piece of formal logic. It describes how a creature’s perception of a situation, together with desire for a good, can issue in movement. For Nussbaum, this becomes a general model of embodied agency: the mind is not first contemplative and then optionally practical; it is practical from the beginning.

The same structure underlies her view of human deliberation. Human practical reason is not a pure calculating faculty set over against passion. It is formed by perception, imagination, emotion, memory, narrative, bodily vulnerability, and social education. Literature matters to ethics because it trains the mind to perceive particulars, imagine the inner lives of others, and respond with appropriate emotion. Love’s Knowledge explicitly concerns the relationship between literature and philosophy, the role of emotion in deliberation and self-knowledge, and the priority of perceiving particular persons and situations over abstract rule-following.

This helps explain why Nussbaum’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from moral epistemology. We know ethically not only by inference, but by trained attention. The morally mature mind sees what is there to be seen: another person’s humiliation, an animal’s distress, a child’s dependency, a minority’s fear, a beloved’s grief.

6. Vulnerability and the structure of the self

The theme of vulnerability is the backbone of Nussbaum’s work. The Fragility of Goodness contrasts Greek tragic and Aristotelian views of human flourishing with Platonic attempts to secure goodness against luck. The issue is whether a good life can be protected from contingency or whether its very goodness depends on attachments exposed to loss.

Nussbaum’s answer is Aristotelian and tragic rather than Platonic: the best human life is not invulnerable. Love, friendship, grief, political commitment, compassion, and moral concern all bind us to goods outside our control. A mind without such vulnerability would not be a perfected human mind but a mutilated one.

This is why emotions are philosophically central. They disclose the world’s hold over us. Grief reveals that another person was not a replaceable source of utility but part of one’s flourishing. Fear reveals perceived danger to something one values. Compassion reveals the perceived significance of another’s suffering. Love reveals an attachment that cannot be fully controlled by the will.

The self, then, is not an isolated atom. It is constituted by attachments. Nussbaum’s eudaimonism does not collapse others into egoistic possessions; rather, it shows that a human life is enlarged by recognizing others as intrinsic parts of one’s practical world. The mind is relational because the self is relational.

This also explains why Nussbaum is suspicious of ethical ideals of emotional extirpation. In an interview, she characterizes a major strand of ancient therapy as seeking to remove emotions that bind us to external goods, whereas her own work treats these attachments as central to justice and human life.

7. Embodiment, disgust, shame, and the denial of animality

Nussbaum’s work on disgust and shame is essential for extracting her philosophy of mind because it shows how mental life can become distorted by fantasies about the body. Hiding from Humanity argues that shame and disgust are dangerous foundations for law because they often express a wish to deny human animality, impurity, dependency, and mortality.

In Phenomenology and Mind, she distinguishes primary disgust from projective disgust. Primary disgust concerns things such as decay or bodily excretion. Projective disgust transfers anxieties about animality, weakness, sex, disability, mortality, or bodily vulnerability onto stigmatized groups. The mind protects itself from its own animal condition by treating other people as pollutants.

This is a philosophical-psychological thesis with political consequences. Disgust is not merely a feeling; it carries an anthropology. It says: “I am not like that. I am pure. They are contaminating.” A society governed by this mental posture will produce exclusion, caste, misogyny, homophobia, ableism, and religious or ethnic stigmatization.

Here the theological implications are serious. Nussbaum opposes forms of moral imagination that equate dignity with escaping the body. Human dignity is not the dignity of disembodiment. It is the dignity of vulnerable, mortal, dependent, needful, desiring, aging, excreting, loving creatures. This is also why some theological readers have found her useful for moral theology: a Theological Studies article argues that her account of emotion helps overcome mind/body and emotion/reason dualisms, and that her emphasis on vulnerability and receptivity has affinities with Christian themes such as grace, even though Aquinas is not a direct source of her theory.

A Spanish-language article in Themata similarly reads Nussbaum’s cognitive-evaluative theory of emotion as a resource for criticizing traditions that separate soul from body, mind from brain, and emotion from reason. This confirms that the mind/body issue is not a marginal implication but one of the central interpretive lines in Nussbaum reception outside Anglophone debate.

8. Human mental powers as capabilities

Nussbaum’s capabilities approach transforms philosophy of mind into political theory. A mind is not merely something one has; it is something whose powers can be cultivated, starved, protected, distorted, or humiliated by social arrangements.

The central capabilities list includes “Senses, Imagination, and Thought,” “Emotions,” “Practical Reason,” “Affiliation,” relations with “Other Species,” “Play,” and “Control over One’s Environment.” These are not incidental welfare goods. They are conditions for a life worthy of human dignity.

This means that Nussbaum’s political philosophy presupposes a thick philosophical psychology. To secure justice is partly to secure the social conditions under which human beings can perceive, imagine, think, love, grieve, attach, deliberate, affiliate, play, and exercise agency. Poverty, caste hierarchy, misogyny, racism, disability exclusion, religious persecution, educational deprivation, and political terror are not only external harms. They deform the development and exercise of mental powers.

The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes the capability approach as focusing on what people are able to do and be—their real opportunities for functionings—and identifies Nussbaum as one of its principal developers alongside Amartya Sen. It also notes the approach’s extension to animals and its engagement with questions of nature, dignity, flourishing, and non-Western traditions.

The capabilities approach therefore contains an implicit theory of mental development. The human mind is plastic but not infinitely plastic. It has characteristic powers, and those powers require material, educational, affective, legal, and cultural support. A starving child, a terrorized minority, a girl denied education, a person shamed for disability, or a prisoner deprived of affiliation has not merely lost external goods. Their very field of perception, desire, self-respect, practical reason, and emotional possibility is being injured.

9. Literature, imagination, and the education of perception

Nussbaum’s literary work supplies another major part of her philosophy of mind. In Love’s Knowledge, she argues that ethical understanding requires emotional as well as intellectual activity and gives priority to fine perception of particular persons and situations over abstract rules.

In Poetic Justice, she argues that literary imagination helps readers grasp the inner quality of other lives and care about their fate. A review of that work summarizes her claim that imaginative engagement with subjective experience is necessary for moral judgment and public citizenship.

This is not aesthetic ornamentation. It is a theory of moral cognition. The mind becomes more just by learning how to imagine. Novels, tragedies, music, and other arts can train attention to the concrete, the fragile, the particular, and the hidden. They help counteract the crude classificatory habits by which societies dehumanize people or ignore animal suffering.

A German discussion of Upheavals of Thought notes that Nussbaum’s large-scale emotion theory continues the program already visible in Love’s Knowledge: literature and art are indispensable for moral philosophy because they cultivate emotionally intelligent perception.

The practical implication is that rationality itself is culturally educated. Good judgment requires not less imagination but better imagination. The danger is sentimental manipulation; the remedy is not emotional austerity but critical, democratic, self-scrutinizing emotional education.

10. Political emotion: fear, anger, compassion, love

Nussbaum’s philosophy of mind is also a political psychology. Political orders do not rest on institutions alone. They require public emotions: love of justice, compassion for suffering, trust, hope, righteous concern, and sometimes disciplined indignation. But they are also threatened by fear, disgust, envy, resentment, shame, and retaliatory anger.

In Political Emotions, she asks how a decent society aspiring to justice can be sustained by public emotions rooted in love and attachment while keeping destructive emotions such as disgust and envy at bay.

Her later work on anger is especially severe. In “Transitional Anger,” she argues that ordinary anger is often normatively irrational because it either involves magical thinking about payback or embodies defective values. The acceptable form is “transitional anger”: anger that moves quickly from the perception of injustice to forward-looking efforts at repair.

This matters for philosophy of mind because Nussbaum treats anger as a mental state with a structure, not merely an outburst. Anger often contains a fantasy: that another person’s suffering can somehow undo the wrong. By exposing that structure, she converts moral psychology into ethical critique.

Fear receives similar treatment. Reviews and discussions of The Monarchy of Fear present fear as a primitive and politically manipulable emotion, often underlying anger, disgust, and envy. Fear can become attached to vulnerable out-groups and can be exploited by demagogues.

The political mind is therefore fragile. It can be educated toward justice, but it can also be captured by fear, purity fantasies, humiliation, and retaliatory imagination. Political liberalism, for Nussbaum, requires not emotional neutrality but disciplined emotional cultivation.

11. Animal minds: sentience, striving, dignity

Nussbaum’s most developed recent philosophy of consciousness appears in her animal ethics. She rejects two inadequate models: an anthropocentric model that values animals mainly when they resemble humans, and a utilitarian model that focuses too narrowly on pleasure and pain. In a Boston Review interview, she says the capabilities approach asks whether sentient animals are enabled by laws and institutions to live decently flourishing lives, each according to its own form of life.

Her account begins from animals as active subjects, not passive containers of pleasure and pain. In “Beyond ‘Compassion and Humanity’: Justice for Nonhuman Animals,” she argues that animals pursue ends, strive toward goods, and can be impeded in their characteristic activities. She combines a Kantian respect for each sentient individual with a neo-Aristotelian attention to diverse species-specific forms of flourishing.

The crucial moral-psychological idea is “significant striving.” A sentient creature has a subjective point of view, perceives the world as meaningful, desires goods, avoids bads, and can be blocked, injured, humiliated, confined, or deprived. Justice requires protecting the creature’s opportunities for characteristic functioning.

This gives Nussbaum a powerful answer to a classic problem in philosophy of mind: how can we know animal minds? She rejects the demand for impossible certainty. We know other humans’ minds through biology, behavior, expression, inference to the best explanation, and imaginative interpretation. The same general methods apply to animals. We do not need proof of human-like language or reflective self-consciousness to recognize sentience, fear, play, attachment, grief, curiosity, and agency.

Her later language is strikingly expansive. She says many animals display reasoning, problem-solving, forms of normativity, and sophisticated emotions; “reason,” in this broader sense, permeates the animal world.

This does not erase differences among species. On the contrary, Nussbaum’s view is pluralist. The flourishing of an elephant, dolphin, dog, crow, pig, whale, horse, octopus, or human being is not identical. A French discussion of her animal ethics stresses that recognizing animal dignity and subjectivity does not mean simply transferring human rights wholesale to animals; it means recognizing vulnerable, sentient beings according to their own forms of life.

A Spanish/Catalan article on Nussbaum’s animal justice argues that the cognitive reach of affectivity allows her to move beyond human-centered ethics: ethical emotions such as wonder, compassion, and transition anger help ground recognition of rights for nonhuman animals. The article also emphasizes that, in Justice for Animals, compassion, outrage, and wonder are decisive for perceiving and responding to injustice toward sentient animal life.

The resulting philosophy of mind is anti-exceptionalist. Human beings are distinctive, but not metaphysically alone. They are one kind of minded animal among many.

12. The “soul” and public metaphysical restraint

Nussbaum’s use of “soul” must be interpreted with care. In her Aristotelian work, soul means psychē: the living organization and capacities of an embodied creature. In her political liberalism, however, she deliberately avoids making controversial metaphysical claims about the existence, immortality, or destiny of the soul part of public doctrine.

In the Phenomenology and Mind interview, she says political principles can express a moral vision while remaining neutral on divisive metaphysical matters such as the existence and destiny of the soul.

This is a key theological-political point. Nussbaum’s public philosophy does not deny religious doctrines about the soul. It brackets them for purposes of political justification. Citizens may affirm immortal souls, divine creation, reincarnation, Buddhist no-self, Jewish covenantal anthropology, Christian imago Dei, Islamic accountability before God, or secular naturalism. The political order should protect conscience and dignity without requiring agreement on these metaphysical doctrines.

This is also why her defense of religious liberty is not a side issue. In her discussions of religious intolerance, she argues for equal respect for conscience, criticizes fear-based treatment of minorities, especially Muslims in post-9/11 contexts, and emphasizes sympathetic imagination as a political virtue.

Her thought therefore contains two different but compatible uses of “soul.” Philosophically, in the Aristotelian register, soul is embodied life-form. Politically, in the liberal register, doctrines about the soul are matters on which citizens may differ, and the state should not impose a sectarian answer.

13. Theological themes: vulnerability, transcendence, conscience, grace

Nussbaum’s theology-adjacent thought is complex. She is not a theologian constructing doctrine, but she is persistently concerned with themes that theology has traditionally claimed: soul, mortality, transcendence, sin-like denial, compassion, conscience, justice, love, and the temptation to escape creatureliness.

Biographically, Nussbaum has described herself as a convert to Judaism and has connected Judaism with moral debate, justice, doubt, and ritual practice. In an interview, she says she found in Judaism a space where outrage at injustice was normal and where argument and debate mattered more than catechism. Later in the same interview, she connects Reform Jewish practice with moral reflection, political action, and even a broadened tikkun olam involving repair of human relations with other species.

Her religious-liberty work treats conscience as central to dignity. In a Bill Moyers interview, she argues that fairness and equality, rather than a simplistic “separation” vocabulary, better capture the political issue; she also says religious traditions have deep resources for thinking about dignity and conscience as the search for life’s meaning.

There is also a critical theological edge. In an interview with The Guardian, the discussion frames her as defending an Aristotelian this-worldly ethics against Platonic or supernatural displacement of justice into another realm. Nussbaum says that “putting the problems of justice into another world” is one of her problems with Christianity, while also acknowledging that Christian ideas such as the image of God can be politically fruitful.

The deeper issue is not hostility to religion as such. It is opposition to any spiritual ideal that treats embodied vulnerability as something to flee. For Nussbaum, a human being should not aspire to become a bloodless god beyond grief, dependency, sexuality, appetite, aging, and loss. The aspiration to invulnerability corrupts both ethics and politics.

This is why Christian theologians have found both tension and resource in her work. The Theological Studies article mentioned earlier argues that Nussbaum and Aquinas share Aristotelian and Stoic background commitments concerning the cognitive orientation of emotions, even though Aquinas locates emotion in the appetitive part of the soul. It also claims that Nussbaum’s account of vulnerability and receptiveness resonates with Christian thinking about grace and helps moral theology attend more honestly to corporeality, dependency, and mortality.

Thus, the theological significance of Nussbaum’s philosophy of mind can be summarized as follows: she rejects disembodied, purity-based, invulnerability-seeking pictures of the soul; she respects religious conscience as a central human capability; she treats love, compassion, fear, and ritual as serious forms of moral psychology; and she invites theology to become more attentive to vulnerable embodied life.

14. Multilingual reception: what non-Anglophone sources add

The non-Anglophone reception does not overturn the main English-language interpretation, but it highlights several emphases that are sometimes less prominent in Anglo-American commentary.

Spanish-language scholarship often stresses the practical and motivational force of emotion. One Spanish article states that for Nussbaum emotions are not inert but evaluative, motivational, and connected with vulnerability and dignity. It treats emotions as helping us perceive moral harm and motivating action.

Another Spanish article is more critical, especially concerning Aristotle. It argues that Nussbaum and related interpreters rightly give emotion a cognitive and practical role in moral perception and education, but it questions whether her judgment-centered account fully captures Aristotle’s position.

Portuguese reception emphasizes intentionality, eudaimonistic judgment, and democratic emotional education. This is helpful because it connects Nussbaum’s philosophy of mind to civic formation: the mind’s affective habits are not politically neutral.

French reception highlights imagination. The French discussion of Upheavals and Love’s Knowledge underscores that emotion involves concrete imaginative presentation, not merely abstract judgment. Another French source on animals emphasizes Nussbaum’s recognition of animal subjectivity and dignity without simply anthropomorphizing animals.

Italian reception usefully stresses that Nussbaum’s emotions are cognitive and intentional without being necessarily voluntary or conscious. That is important for avoiding a simplistic reading in which “judgment” means deliberate, explicit assent.

German reception tends to emphasize the scale of Upheavals of Thought as a programmatic defense of the intelligence of emotions and the role of literature and art in moral philosophy. A German theological-social-ethical discussion of the capabilities approach also highlights that Nussbaum’s method avoids both a merely biological account of human nature and a dogmatic metaphysical-theological foundation, while still relying on an explicitly normative conception of human capabilities.

The multilingual picture sharpens the reconstruction in three ways: it makes the mind/body issue more visible; it foregrounds imagination as a cognitive power; and it shows how animal ethics, theology, and democratic education are natural extensions of her moral psychology rather than separate topics.

15. Critical tensions in the reconstructed philosophy of mind

A rigorous reconstruction should not hide the pressure points.

The first is over-cognitivism. If emotions are judgments, what about preverbal infants, trauma responses, panic, depression, animal fear, moods, bodily anxiety, or recalcitrant emotion that persists despite contrary belief? Nussbaum has resources for answering this: judgment can be nonlinguistic, imagistic, habitual, and not fully conscious. But critics are right that “judgment” can sound too intellectualist unless carefully broadened. The Spanish critique of her Aristotelian reading is especially relevant here.

The second tension concerns embodiment. Nussbaum rejects mind/body dualism, yet her emphasis on evaluative content can sometimes seem to subordinate bodily affect to cognition. The best charitable reading is that she is not denying bodily feeling but denying that bodily feeling alone individuates emotions. Still, philosophers influenced by phenomenology, affect theory, psychoanalysis, or neuroscience may want a richer account of bodily temporality, unconscious affect, and preconceptual mood.

The third tension concerns metaphysics. Nussbaum often presents the capabilities approach as political rather than metaphysical. Yet the approach plainly depends on claims about human and animal flourishing, dignity, and characteristic powers. German theological-social-ethical discussion rightly notes that her method avoids dogmatic metaphysics but still rests on normative anthropological claims.

The fourth tension concerns animals and boundaries. Nussbaum draws the line at sentience and significant striving, not at life as such. This excludes plants and perhaps some borderline organisms from direct justice claims, though she allows that classifications should change as scientific understanding improves. Her own animal justice article is cautious: it identifies mammals, birds, and teleost fish as relatively secure cases, while treating other cases as more difficult and open to future discovery.

The fifth tension concerns theology. Nussbaum’s public philosophy respects religious conscience, but her moral anthropology is wary of transcendence when transcendence becomes escape from vulnerability. Some Christian, Platonic, Hindu, Buddhist, or Islamic thinkers may argue that she underestimates spiritual disciplines that seek transformation without denying embodied compassion. Others will find her account deeply compatible with incarnational, Jewish, Aristotelian-Thomist, liberationist, or feminist theological emphases on embodied justice.

16. A systematic Nussbaumian philosophy of mind: extracted theses

The following theses give the most compact systematic formulation of the philosophy of mind implicit in Nussbaum’s work.

First, soul is embodied life-organization. The Aristotelian psychē is not a detachable ghost but the form and activity of a living body.

Second, mind is teleological. Mental states are intelligible in relation to the goods, needs, vulnerabilities, and characteristic activities of a creature.

Third, perception is not neutral registration. It is already practically and affectively oriented toward a world of significance.

Fourth, consciousness is subjective point of view. A creature is sentient when the world appears or feels some way to it.

Fifth, metacognition is not the criterion of consciousness. Reflective self-awareness is only one advanced form of mindedness; pain, fear, desire, pleasure, distress, curiosity, and attachment need not require it.

Sixth, desire is a felt orientation toward goods. Agency begins from the creature’s active striving, not from detached calculation.

Seventh, emotion is evaluative intelligence. Emotions are intentional appraisals of what matters for flourishing, not irrational eruptions opposed to reason.

Eighth, imagination is a cognitive power. It mediates ethical perception, emotional understanding, literature, sympathy, and the recognition of other minds.

Ninth, practical reason is affectively constituted. Good deliberation requires trained emotion, perception, memory, imagination, and attention to particulars.

Tenth, the self is vulnerable and relational. A flourishing person is bound to external goods—beloved persons, communities, animals, political ideals, and fragile projects—that cannot be fully controlled.

Eleventh, embodiment is not a humiliation. Disgust and shame become morally dangerous when they express fantasies of purity and denial of animality.

Twelfth, minds are socially formed. Education, law, poverty, stigma, literature, ritual, family, political culture, and public emotion shape what people can perceive, imagine, feel, and choose.

Thirteenth, justice protects mental capabilities. A just society secures the conditions for thought, imagination, emotion, affiliation, practical reason, play, and meaningful agency.

Fourteenth, animal minds are real and morally central. Many animals are sentient subjects with species-specific forms of striving and flourishing.

Fifteenth, dignity is plural and life-form-relative. Human dignity is not the only dignity; animal dignity must be understood according to each creature’s characteristic powers and vulnerabilities.

Sixteenth, religion is philosophically serious but politically noncoercive. Conscience and religious meaning are central human capabilities, but public principles should not depend on sectarian doctrines about the soul’s ultimate destiny.

Seventeenth, fantasies of invulnerability are ethically corrupting. Whether secular or religious, the wish to escape dependency, grief, mortality, and bodily need damages moral perception.

Eighteenth, the mature mind is not detached but rightly attached. Nussbaum’s ideal is not apathy but emotionally intelligent openness governed by justice.

Conclusion

Martha Nussbaum’s philosophy of mind is not a theory of the mind as an isolated epistemic machine. It is a theory of minded life. Its basic unit is the vulnerable creature striving in a world that matters.

From Aristotle she takes the idea that soul is embodied form and that perception, desire, imagination, and movement belong to the living animal as a whole. From tragedy and Aristotle she takes the idea that human goodness is fragile because it depends on external goods. From Stoic and anti-Stoic debates she develops the claim that emotions are intelligent appraisals, while rejecting the ideal of emotional extirpation. From literature she takes the idea that imagination educates moral perception. From political liberalism she takes the need to protect conscience without imposing metaphysical doctrine. From animal ethics she derives her most explicit account of sentience as subjective, affective, striving consciousness.

The resulting view is best described as an embodied, evaluative, affective, Aristotelian, capability-based, anti-Cartesian philosophy of mind. Its core insight is that consciousness is inseparable from significance. To have a mind is not merely to process information. It is to inhabit a world as meaningful, dangerous, lovable, grievable, usable, resistible, and worth striving in.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
